The Frick Collection (also known as the Frick) is an amazing and well renowned art museum, and one of the preeminent destinations of New York City!!!

Based on the Upper East Side of Manhattan, New York, the museum is located inside the Henry Clay Frick House, a breathtaking Beaux-Arts mansion that was designed for Henry Clay Frick. Frick was a prominent 20th Century American industrialist, financier, art patron, and business partner of the wealthy tycoon and philanthropist, Andrew Carnegie.

The Henry Clay Frick House was designed by renowned architect, Thomas Hastings and its construction was completed in 1914. Due to its distinctive design and its pioneering architectural features, the mansion is listed on the National Register Of Historic Places and is recognized as a US National Historic Landmark.

In accordance with Frick’s will, the mansion would be converted to a museum and opened to the public in 1935. The museum features Frick’s extensive and immaculate collection of art which consists of paintings, ceramics, and sculptures. Due to Frick’s admiration of European art spanning from the 14th to 19th Century, guests will have an opportunity to view works or art made by artists such as Giovanni Bellini, Rembrandt, Diego Velasquez, Johannes Vermeer, Thomas Gainsborough and many others.

After the extensive renovation that took place between 2021-2025, visitors and patrons are now able explore to the mansion’s 2nd floor and have access to viewing multiple galleries of art, shopping for merchandise at the museum’s gift shop as well as visiting and dining at the museum’s cafe.

In July of 2025, I brought and treated my wife and myself for a self guided tour of the Frick collection and we very much enjoyed our experience. In light of our membership to the museum, we have priority access and can enter the Frick without any reservations required. I highly recommend a purchase of the membership pass to access these benefits and so much more.

During our visit of the Frick, my wife and I explored the “West Gallery” which is recognized as the “crown jewel attraction” of the museum. The “West Gallery” is known for its contemplative atmosphere, impressive lighting system, and its iconic collection of works of art. We enjoyed viewing Rembrandt’s work such as the “Self-Potrait” and Johannes Vermeer’s potraits, such as the “Officer and Laughing Girl” & the “ Mistress & Maid.” Guests visiting the “West Gallery” will also be able to enjoy works by Frans Hals and J.M.W. Turner.

Another room that we enjoyed visiting was the Fragonard Room. The Fragonard Room contains our favorite work of art, “The Progress Of Love.” The Progress Of Love” is a series of gorgeous paintings made by Jean-Honoré Fragonard which depicts love and courtship in its various stages, such as the initial encounter and the couple’s eventual acceptance and love for each other. We loved this artwork due to the various themes that one can ascertain when looking at the piece, such as shared resilience, the importance for lovers to learn from each other, and work with each other to overcome adversity.

Other works of art that we loved viewing was Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres”s painting, “The Portrait of Comtesse d'Haussonville” the “St Francis in the Desert” created by Italian Renaissance master Giovanni Bellini, and the portrait of “Henry Frick developed by John C. Johansen. 

Last but certainly not least, both my wife and I had fun exploring the Frick’s indoor courtyard, the Garden Court. The Garden Court is quite mesmerizing due to its feature of majestic columns, and a central water fountain surrounded by greenery and stunning ornate statues.

If you haven’t done so already, be sure to stop by and visit the Frick Collection. You’ll have a fun time when exploring this outstanding destination!!!

What an Opulent Museum Setting! How fun to imagine oneself in the early 1900s, walking in the Frick Mansion and enjoying all of the artwork a century ago. Advanced tickets are a must. And even with a set amount of tickets, the museum was packed. Be prepared for lines for the coat room, for the bathrooms, and some of the gallery rooms. However, the collection is a feast for the eyes and very worth the slow shuffle to get into the rooms. And it sure seemed that the collection contained "one of everybody." In other words, pretty much any artist you can name (especially the Impressionists) have one painting on display here. This writer regrets to inform you that photography is not allowed in the museum, so I have no photos to share. What I do have is fond memories. For example, on the landing atop a wide wooden staircase, we found a bench upon which we sat for a good ten minutes and just admired a Renoir painting of a woman and two little girls with big, soupy doe eyes. Between a visit to the Frick Collection and the Morgan Library, you will feel as if you are in a time capsule, living in the lap of 20th century luxury in NYC. I am very glad I had the opportunity to visit this museum.

Very impressive. A former private villa that now presents an art collection of the former villa owner as a foundation. There are a total of artworks worth billions hanging there. Incredibly beautiful.
